scope: Gap-fill batch: Chechnya (evolution of radical Islam between the two wars), Russian federal-subject typology (1990s baseline), ethnic composition of Russia and Ukraine at Soviet collapse, Soviet-collapse theories (Dallin institutional (in)coherence + Treisman's economic-crisis thesis + counterfactuals + weaknesses of the nationalism-as-primary-cause view), and 1990s parliamentary elections previously thin in the bank (1995 Russian Duma, 1994 and 1998 Ukrainian Rada).
notes_for_later_batches: Questions about the 2000s/2010s+ shifts in Russia's federal-subject count — okrug-into-krai mergers (2003-2008), 2014 illegal annexation of Crimea and Sevastopol, and the 2022 unrecognized claims over Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, and Kherson oblasts — are deliberately deferred to a later batch focused on 2000s-plus content. When those questions are drafted, the prompt and explanation MUST make clear that the 2014 and 2022 annexations are illegal under international law and in the 2022 cases the territory is not even fully under Russian military control.

questions.prompt: When Dzhokhar Dudayev declared Chechnya's independence in November 1991, his movement is best characterized as:

choices.text: A secular ethno-nationalist project led by a Soviet Air Force general, centered on Chechen sovereignty rather than Islamic revival
A Wahhabi-led Islamist insurgency aimed from the start at founding a Caucasian Islamic emirate
choices.note: Wahhabi and jihadist currents became politically significant in Chechnya only in the mid-to-late 1990s, especially around Shamil Basayev and the Saudi-born commander Khattab. Dudayev himself was a Soviet-trained general whose 1991 independence declaration was framed in secular-nationalist, not religious, terms.

choices.text: A pan-Caucasian confederal movement uniting Chechens, Georgians, and Armenians under a shared anti-Russian platform
choices.note: There was no such tripartite confederal project. Georgian and Armenian political movements pursued their own republican independence paths and did not merge with Dudayev's Chechen bid.

choices.text: A Russian-monarchist restoration movement seeking to reinstall the Romanov dynasty in the North Caucasus
choices.note: Monarchism was peripheral to all the post-Soviet secessionist movements and was certainly not the basis of Dudayev's Chechen project.

questions.explanation: Dudayev seized power in Grozny in the wake of the August 1991 coup and declared Chechnya's independence from the RSFSR on 1 November 1991 — while the USSR still existed. His biography (Soviet Air Force general, fluent Russian-speaker, married to an ethnic Russian) and his early rhetoric were secular-nationalist, aimed at translating the Soviet-era 'autonomous republic' status of Checheno-Ingushetia into full statehood. Religious radicalism became a defining feature of the Chechen conflict only later — during and especially after the First Chechen War (1994-96) — as Wahhabi networks and foreign fighters like Khattab arrived and aligned with Shamil Basayev. Reading the 1991 declaration backwards through the lens of the late-1990s Caucasus Emirate project misdates the religious turn.

questions.prompt: Between the Khasavyurt ceasefire of August 1996 and the renewed war in autumn 1999, the religious and ideological character of the Chechen insurgency shifted most notably because:

choices.text: Wahhabi networks around Basayev and the Saudi-born commander Khattab eclipsed the secular-nationalist authority of president Aslan Maskhadov, culminating in the August 1999 incursion into Dagestan
Maskhadov personally embraced a Wahhabi agenda and dissolved Chechnya's secular institutions, consolidating a unified Islamist republic that then attacked Russia
choices.note: Maskhadov, elected in 1997, tried to preserve a secular-nationalist Chechen statehood and increasingly lost control to Basayev–Khattab field commanders. The August 1999 Dagestan incursion was launched against, not by, his authority.

choices.text: Kremlin-backed local clerics converted most of the Chechen population to Russian Orthodoxy, provoking a Muslim backlash
choices.note: There was no such mass conversion project. The Russian state's post-1999 strategy worked with traditional Sufi figures, such as Akhmat Kadyrov (Chechnya's former chief mufti) who switched to the federal side in November 1999, not through Orthodox proselytization.

choices.text: The United States dispatched special forces to train Chechen militias in evangelical-Christian ideology, alienating the population from Moscow
choices.note: No such US training mission existed. The North Caucasus insurgency drew foreign fighters and money from Gulf-based Salafi networks, not from the West.

questions.explanation: The 1996-99 interwar period saw de-facto-independent Chechnya fragment: Maskhadov held the presidency but field commanders around Basayev and Khattab built autonomous Salafi-oriented armed networks, hostage economies, and sharia courts. In August 1999 Basayev and Khattab led an armed incursion into neighboring Dagestan, which — together with the September 1999 apartment bombings in Moscow, Buynaksk and Volgodonsk — gave new prime minister Vladimir Putin the pretext to launch the Second Chechen War. The long Russian counterinsurgency that followed pursued a 'Chechenization' strategy: backing Akhmat Kadyrov (and later his son Ramzan) against the Wahhabi-leaning fighters, while those fighters themselves increasingly reframed the struggle as a jihad for a Caucasus Emirate rather than Chechen statehood per se.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev, ch. on Chechnya

questions.prompt: At the moment the Russian Federation emerged from the Soviet collapse in late 1991, what types of 'federal subjects' did the new federation contain, and roughly how many were there?

choices.text: 89 federal subjects in six categories: 21 ethnic republics, 49 oblasts, 6 krays, 10 autonomous okrugs, 1 autonomous oblast, and 2 federal cities
Exactly 50 federal subjects, each modeled on a US-style state, with no distinction between ethnic and non-ethnic units
choices.note: The Russian federation inherited a Soviet-era layered structure with several legally distinct types (republics, oblasts, krays, autonomous okrugs, an autonomous oblast, federal cities). The 1990s count was 89, not 50, and units were not uniform.

choices.text: About 200 federal subjects, with every district and municipality counted separately
choices.note: Municipalities and districts (rayons) sit below the federal-subject tier. At the top level there were 89 federal subjects in the 1990s, not 200.

choices.text: A unitary structure with no federal subjects, administered by seven Kremlin-appointed governors-general
choices.note: The 1993 Constitution explicitly defined Russia as a federation with enumerated federal subjects. 'Presidential envoys' heading seven federal districts were introduced by Putin in 2000 and sat alongside — not in place of — the federal-subject system.

questions.explanation: The 1993 Constitution inherited the Soviet-era typology of territorial units. At independence the Russian Federation contained 89 federal subjects split across six categories: 21 ethnic republics (each with its own constitution and titular language); 49 oblasts ('provinces'); 6 krays (oblast-equivalents in frontier or historically peripheral regions); 10 autonomous okrugs, most nested inside krays or oblasts; 1 autonomous oblast (the Jewish Autonomous Oblast in the Far East); and 2 federal cities (Moscow and St. Petersburg). For most political-science purposes oblasts, krays, and okrugs function similarly — the main legally salient distinction is between ethnic republics (with titular-nationality institutions) and the rest. The count shifted in later decades through voluntary okrug-into-krai mergers (2003-2008) and, separately, through the 2014 and 2022 claims of Ukrainian territory, which are not recognized by most of the international community and fall outside the 1990s baseline described here.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return, ch. 8 (federalism and ethnic republics)

questions.prompt: Compared with Russia's oblasts and krays, what made the ethnic republics of the 1990s Russian Federation legally distinctive?

choices.text: Each ethnic republic had its own constitution, a titular nationality, one or more official languages alongside Russian, and a directly elected president
Ethnic republics were administered directly from the Kremlin with no elected leadership, while oblasts and krays had elected governors
choices.note: The opposite: in the 1990s ethnic republics had elected presidents, while governors of oblasts and krays were initially appointed by Yeltsin and only later became elected. Putin-era reforms abolished direct gubernatorial elections in 2004 (briefly reinstated in some forms after 2012), but ethnic-republic presidents were a 1990s distinctive feature.

choices.text: Ethnic republics alone had the constitutional right to secede from the Russian Federation
choices.note: No Russian federal subject has a constitutional right to secede. Several republics in the 1990s (notably Tatarstan) negotiated wide autonomy through bilateral power-sharing treaties, and Chechnya attempted unilateral secession and was met with force. Secession as a constitutional right was a Soviet-level feature of union republics, not a Russian-Federation feature of ethnic republics.

choices.text: Ethnic republics were exempt from Russian federal taxation and kept all revenues locally
choices.note: Some republics (notably Tatarstan under the 1994 bilateral treaty) negotiated unusually favorable revenue-sharing arrangements, but full tax exemption was not their legal status. Russian federal taxes applied in ethnic republics as elsewhere, with negotiated asymmetries at the margins.

questions.explanation: The 21 ethnic republics (Tatarstan, Bashkortostan, Sakha-Yakutia, Komi, Karelia, Chechnya before and during its 1990s conflicts, etc.) were the most institutionally distinctive tier of the 1990s federation: each had its own constitution, its own titular language(s) alongside Russian, and its own directly-elected president. Oblasts and krays had governors, not presidents, and lacked a titular-nationality institutional architecture. A 2010 federal law required ethnic republics to retitle their executives (Tatarstan resisted longest, keeping the 'president' title until 2015), and a series of reforms under Putin further aligned republics with the other federal subjects on appointment procedures. An important caveat: the 'ethnic' republics were not necessarily ethnically homogeneous — Stalin-era resettlement left many with very large ethnic-Russian shares, and Tatars, Bashkirs, and others were themselves minorities in some titular republics.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the ethnic composition of the Russian Federation as it emerged from the Soviet collapse, using 1989 Soviet census data for the RSFSR?

choices.text: Roughly 82 percent ethnic Russian, with more than 100 recognized nationalities; the largest non-Russian group was Tatars at around 4 percent, followed by Ukrainians and smaller groups (Bashkirs, Chuvash, Chechens, Armenians, others)

Around 97 percent ethnic Russian, with all non-Russian minorities together under 3 percent of the population — making the Russian Federation one of the most ethnically homogeneous states in Eurasia at independence
choices.note: Ethnic Russians were the large majority but at around 82 percent, not 97 percent. Non-Russians together were roughly one-fifth of the population, not a trace minority, and were concentrated in the 21 ethnic republics and the autonomous okrugs.

choices.text: A near-even split between ethnic Russians and non-Russians, each about half of the population
choices.note: The RSFSR/Russian Federation was overwhelmingly Russian in 1989 — around 82 percent. A near-even ethnic split described some individual republics (Tatarstan, Kazakhstan), not Russia as a whole.

choices.text: About 60 percent ethnic Russian, with a single dominant non-Russian group (Tatars) at around 30 percent and smaller minorities making up the rest
choices.note: Tatars were the largest non-Russian group but at only around 4 percent of the Russian Federation's population, not 30 percent. The 60/30 profile does not match any post-Soviet country's demography.

questions.explanation: The 1989 Soviet census recorded the RSFSR — which became the Russian Federation in 1991 — as roughly 81-82 percent ethnic Russian, with 105 recognized nationalities of more than 1,000 members. Tatars were the largest non-Russian group at approximately 3.8 percent, followed by Ukrainians (around 3 percent), then Chuvash, Bashkirs, Belarusians, and (among North Caucasian peoples) Chechens and others. For context, the Soviet Union as a whole was far less Russian-dominant — ethnic Russians were roughly 50-51 percent of the USSR's 287 million people in 1989 — so the creation of the Russian Federation out of the RSFSR concentrated a much higher ethnic-Russian share into a single successor state. The minorities that remained were concentrated geographically in the ethnic republics and autonomous okrugs, which made minority politics institutionally salient even where each group was small at the all-Russia level.

questions.prompt: How should the demographic composition of Russia's ethnic republics be characterized in the 1990s?

choices.text: Wide variation: titular nationalities ranged from outright majorities (Chechens, Tuvans) to minorities within their own republic (Karelians, Komi), with ethnic Russians often the plurality — a legacy of Soviet-era resettlement
Uniformly titular-majority: each ethnic republic was at least 75 percent its own titular nationality, reflecting Soviet policies of concentrating each people on its own territory
choices.note: Titular nationalities varied widely as a share of republic populations — from clear majorities in some (Chechnya, Tuva) to minorities in others (Karelia, Komi). A uniform 75 percent threshold does not match the 1989 or later census data.

choices.text: Uniformly ethnic-Russian majority: every ethnic republic had become more than 60 percent ethnic Russian by 1989, leaving titular nationalities as demographic minorities everywhere
choices.note: Some republics were indeed majority-Russian, but many (Chechnya, Dagestan, Tuva, Ingushetia, Kabardino-Balkaria, etc.) had titular-nationality majorities or pluralities. Uniformity in either direction is not supported by the data.

choices.text: Essentially identical across republics, with each containing roughly 40 percent of its titular nationality, 40 percent ethnic Russians, and 20 percent others
choices.note: Republic-by-republic composition varied enormously — some republics were 80-90 percent titular, others 10-20 percent titular. A common demographic template did not exist.

questions.explanation: Despite the 'ethnic republic' label, 1989 census data show wide variation across the 21 republics. In Tatarstan, the titular Tatars were a narrow plurality (around 48 percent) with ethnic Russians close behind (around 43 percent). In Karelia, Komi, Udmurtia, and Buryatia, ethnic Russians were actually the largest group. At the other end, Chechnya, Tuva, Ingushetia, and Dagestan had strong titular-nationality majorities. Stalin-era resettlement and industrial migration into titular territories explain much of this variation, along with deportations (notably of Chechens in 1944) and differential birth rates. The demographic heterogeneity helps explain the politics of 1990s asymmetric federalism: republic leaders who used titular-nationalist rhetoric often had to bargain within electorates where ethnic Russians were a large constituency, which pushed many toward negotiated autonomy rather than secession.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the ethnic composition of Ukraine at the time of independence in 1991?

choices.text: Around 73 percent ethnic Ukrainian and 22 percent ethnic Russian, with smaller Belarusian, Moldovan, and Crimean Tatar communities; ethnic Russians were concentrated in Crimea, the industrial east, and port cities
Around 95 percent ethnic Ukrainian with only tiny ethnic minorities, making Ukraine one of the most ethnically homogeneous states in Europe at independence
choices.note: Ethnic Russians alone were more than one-fifth of Ukraine's 1989 census population, concentrated in Crimea and the east. Ukraine was significantly less ethnically homogeneous than this claim suggests.

choices.text: Roughly a 50-50 split between ethnic Ukrainians and ethnic Russians, with the east and west almost identical in ethnic composition
choices.note: Ethnic Ukrainians were a substantial majority — around 73 percent — not a mere plurality. And ethnic composition varied sharply by region, with ethnic Russians much more concentrated in Crimea, the Donbas, and port cities than in western Ukraine.

choices.text: Crimean Tatars made up the plurality of Ukraine's population at independence, with ethnic Ukrainians a close second and ethnic Russians a small minority
choices.note: Crimean Tatars were a small minority concentrated in Crimea — not a plurality of Ukraine. Stalin's 1944 deportation had cut their Crimean presence drastically, and only after 1989 did many return; at independence they numbered in the hundreds of thousands, nationwide under 1 percent.

questions.explanation: The 1989 Soviet census recorded Ukraine as roughly 73 percent ethnic Ukrainian, 22 percent ethnic Russian, with smaller Jewish, Belarusian, Moldovan, Polish, and Hungarian communities, plus the returning Crimean Tatar population in Crimea. The regional distribution was politically decisive: ethnic Russians were a majority in Crimea (around 66 percent of the peninsula in 1989) and formed very large shares of the populations in Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv, and Odesa, while western Ukraine (Galicia, Volhynia, Bukovyna) was overwhelmingly ethnic Ukrainian. This uneven ethnic geography, layered on top of an even more uneven linguistic geography, structured Ukrainian politics throughout the 1990s — and later became the terrain on which Russian claims in 2014 and 2022 were argued.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: Why did ethnicity and first language NOT line up neatly for many Ukrainians in the 1990s?

choices.text: Soviet-era Russification meant many ethnic Ukrainians — especially in cities, industrial regions, and the east and south — used Russian as their first language, so self-identified ethnic Ukrainians outnumbered Ukrainian-native-language speakers
Ukrainians who lived in the countryside overwhelmingly spoke Russian, while Ukrainians who lived in cities overwhelmingly spoke Ukrainian
choices.note: The pattern was closer to the reverse: Ukrainian was more dominant in rural and western areas, Russian more dominant in industrial and urban areas and in the east and south. The rural-Ukrainian / urban-Russophone pattern was the common mismatch.

choices.text: A 1985 Soviet-era Ukrainian SSR language law required ethnic Ukrainians in urban industrial centers to register Russian as their working language on internal-passport documents, artificially inflating Russian-language statistics in late-Soviet and early post-Soviet censuses
choices.note: No such 1985 registration law existed. Soviet internal passports recorded natsionalnost (ethnicity), not first language, and self-reports were not tied to workplace location. The language-ethnicity gap in Ukraine came from long-run Russification incentives (school of instruction, urban migration, career paths), not from a specific 1985 statute.

choices.text: Sustained labor migration from Russia into eastern Ukraine after 1991 rapidly Russified parts of the population, generating new ethnic Ukrainians whose first language remained Russian
choices.note: The language-ethnicity gap was already visible in the 1989 Soviet census — before any post-1991 migration could have produced it — and reflects ethnic Ukrainians who grew up using Russian under late-Soviet conditions, not post-independence demographic flows.

questions.explanation: Decades of Soviet Russification — professional advancement through Russian, Russian-medium schooling in many urban areas, and internal migration — meant that many ethnic Ukrainians grew up speaking Russian at home and work while still identifying as Ukrainian on census forms. The result is a systematic gap between ethnic identification (around 73 percent Ukrainian in 1989) and first-language identification (a considerably lower share reporting Ukrainian as native language, with substantial 'Russophone Ukrainians' in between). That mismatch is central to why post-Soviet Ukraine's internal politics has tended to divide along linguistic and regional lines rather than strictly ethnic ones, and why concepts like 'ethnic Russian in Ukraine' versus 'Russian-speaking Ukrainian' are analytically distinct rather than interchangeable.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States (ch. on identity and language)

questions.prompt: Which statement best characterizes the nation-building approach adopted by the Ukrainian state in its first decade of independence?

choices.text: Inclusive territory-based citizenship at independence, removal of the Soviet 'nationality' passport entry, and a 2001 parties law requiring support from two-thirds of districts in two-thirds of oblasts

A restrictive citizenship regime granting automatic citizenship only to ethnic Ukrainians, with ethnic Russians required to naturalize through a Ukrainian-language test and formal renunciation of Russian ties
choices.note: Ukraine's 1991 citizenship law was notably inclusive: all permanent residents on Ukrainian territory at independence received citizenship regardless of ethnicity. No differential ethnic track and no mass naturalization test for ethnic Russians existed.

choices.text: Adoption of a Russian-style ethnic-republics model inside Ukraine, giving each major minority (Russians, Hungarians, Crimean Tatars, Romanians/Moldovans) its own autonomous territory with titular institutions
choices.note: Ukraine never adopted an ethnic-republics model. The only autonomous republic is the (territorially, not ethnically, defined) Autonomous Republic of Crimea. Other minorities had no titular territorial units.

choices.text: A constitutional declaration of two co-equal state nationalities (Ukrainian and Russian), with parliamentary seats allocated by ethnic quota and Russian granted equal-state-language status nationwide in the 1996 Constitution
choices.note: The 1996 Constitution named Ukrainian as the sole state language and did not impose ethnic quotas in parliament. A formal dual-state-nationality framework was never adopted; Russian received special status in some regions only temporarily, under the 2012 Yanukovych-era language law later repealed.

questions.explanation: Ukraine's nation-building choices in the 1990s leaned civic-territorial rather than ethno-titular. At independence it adopted a 'zero-option' citizenship extended to all permanent residents regardless of ethnicity, and the new Ukrainian passport dropped the Soviet 'fifth-point' nationality entry. The 2001 Law on Political Parties reinforced the same logic by requiring party registration to show nationwide support — 10,000 signatures drawn from at least two-thirds of Ukraine's rayons in two-thirds of its oblasts — which in practice prevented ethnic-minority or regionally concentrated parties (a Crimean-Tatar or Transcarpathian-Hungarian party, for example) from registering as national actors. Identity politics in Ukraine continued to operate along linguistic and regional lines, but formal institutions kept them out of the ethnic-titular grooves that shape Russian, Baltic, and Caucasus politics.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: In Alexander Dallin's framework on the Soviet collapse, the 'institutional (in)coherence' argument holds that:

choices.text: Soviet totalitarianism rested on interlocking institutions — terror, command economy, ideological monopoly, and Party primacy — that propped each other up; once any of them weakened, the rest could not hold (Dallin's Jenga metaphor)
The Soviet collapse was caused almost entirely by a single external factor — the US military buildup of the early 1980s — that structurally outcompeted the Soviet economy
choices.note: That is closer to Dallin's 'impact of external environment' category, which he treats as one structural hypothesis among several (and against which he notes the timing doesn't match — US pressure had peaked by 1986). 'Institutional incoherence' is a distinct argument that lives between structure and agency.

choices.text: Collapse was the deterministic product of nationalism alone: once any republic demanded sovereignty, the rest inevitably followed in sequence
choices.note: That is the 'nationalist revival' argument, which Dallin and others treat as partial — noting that most 1991 republic leaders were longtime Party members and that ethnic-Russian populations in the Baltics often backed local sovereignty, which complicates a pure-nationalism story.

choices.text: The collapse was caused by Gorbachev personally and would not have occurred under a different General Secretary, making it a purely contingent, not structural, event
choices.note: That is closer to the pure contingent/agency-centric argument. Dallin's institutional-incoherence argument operates between structural and contingent — Gorbachev's choices matter, but they matter because they removed specific interlocking pillars of the system.

questions.explanation: In his article 'Causes of the Soviet Collapse,' Alexander Dallin locates the event neither in a single long-run structural cause nor in pure leader agency, but in the loosening of interlocking institutional supports. The system relied on mutually reinforcing pillars — mass terror, ideological monopoly, command economy, and the Party's leading role (and, as an institutional feature distinct from totalitarianism proper, the Soviet federation itself). De-Stalinization relaxed terror; late-Brezhnev corruption and elite cynicism eroded ideology; perestroika and democratization cut into Party primacy; glasnost and republic-level elections then animated nationalist mobilization within the federation. Each change on its own might have been survivable, but because the pillars had propped one another up, removing them in sequence left nothing holding the system in place.
sources: Alexander Dallin, 'Causes of the Soviet Collapse' (assigned reading for Lectures 3 & 4)

questions.prompt: Daniel Treisman's explanation of the Soviet collapse in The Return emphasizes that:

choices.text: The 1990 acute economic crisis (shortages, rationing, food riots) and fear of mass hunger eroded popular support for Gorbachev and swung Russians toward Yeltsin
The collapse was caused primarily by the moral illegitimacy of Soviet totalitarianism, which the public had rejected from the 1960s onward
choices.note: Treisman argues, citing 1980s polling, that popular disaffection with the Party only set in sharply around 1990 — not from the 1960s. Long-run 'inhumanity' arguments fit poorly with the sudden shift in public support that Treisman documents.

choices.text: The decisive factor was Ronald Reagan's 1983 Strategic Defense Initiative, which bankrupted Soviet military budgets and forced surrender in the Cold War
choices.note: Treisman, like Dallin, is skeptical that the arms race carried the weight required; US military pressure had peaked by 1986 and Soviet reform kept moving regardless. The core of his argument is about domestic economic crisis and public opinion, not about US defense spending.

choices.text: Collapse followed from a unified intellectual project by the Russian nationalist right to dismantle the Party in order to restore a Tsarist monarchy
choices.note: No such unified monarchist project drove the 1990-91 collapse. The decisive political coalition that pulled the system apart was a reformist-marketization-plus-Russian-sovereignty coalition around Yeltsin, not a monarchist one.

questions.explanation: In The Return (chapter 1), Daniel Treisman marshals Soviet-era polling and economic indicators to argue that popular support for Gorbachev and for the system as a whole held up through 1989 and only collapsed in 1990, when rationing, shortages, riots over goods, a 9 percent fall in production, and rapidly rising prices produced a real fear of hunger. That shift — rather than long-run stagnation or moral rejection of totalitarianism — is what turned Yeltsin's push for Russian sovereignty and rapid marketization from a minority position into a broadly supported one (his approval jumped from around 27 percent in 1989 to around 70 percent by late 1990). Treisman speculates that a serious stabilization program in 1985-87 might have saved the system; the question had always been whether the military would use force to hold the USSR together, and in the event it did not.

questions.prompt: Dallin's view on the counterfactual 'What if Gorbachev had never come to power?' is best summarized as:

choices.text: Structural problems were real but slow-moving; without a reformer of Gorbachev's type, the system could have persisted in a decaying form for several more decades rather than collapsing in 1991
The USSR was mathematically certain to collapse by 1992 regardless of who led it, so Gorbachev's choices had no causal weight
choices.note: Dallin is explicitly skeptical of strong structural determinism on timing. He argues that without a reformer of Gorbachev's type, decline was compatible with prolonged survival rather than near-term collapse.

choices.text: Gorbachev was irrelevant because the USSR would have collapsed faster under a hardline successor to Chernenko due to military overreach in Afghanistan
choices.note: Dallin does not argue that a hardliner would have accelerated collapse. His counterfactual runs in the opposite direction: without a reformer, the system's decay could have persisted longer, not shorter.

choices.text: Dallin claims Gorbachev's individual psychology is the only variable that matters, so structural factors can be ignored
choices.note: Dallin does not dismiss structural factors; his institutional-incoherence argument treats them as the preconditions on which Gorbachev's reform choices acted. The counterfactual highlights agency alongside structure, not agency alone.

questions.explanation: In 'Causes of the Soviet Collapse,' Dallin explicitly combines a strong role for long-run institutional erosion with a meaningful role for Gorbachev's particular reform choices — especially his decision to break the Party's monopoly on political truth by releasing Sakharov, legalizing glasnost, and, most consequentially, putting contested elections and the Congress of People's Deputies in place. Without a reformer willing to take those steps, Dallin argues, the USSR's real problems could have been managed (badly) for decades more. The claim is not that Gorbachev 'caused' collapse in a narrow agent-centric sense, but that the timing and form of collapse were not fixed in advance by structure.
sources: Alexander Dallin, 'Causes of the Soviet Collapse'

questions.prompt: What complicates the argument that 'nationalist revival' was the primary cause of the Soviet collapse?

choices.text: Most 1991 republic leaders were longtime CPSU apparatchiks, ethnic Russians in some non-Russian republics backed local sovereignty, and pro-union support flipped from ~70 percent in March 1991 to pro-independence majorities within months
Nationalism could not have been a factor because the Soviet census did not record ethnicity or nationality
choices.note: Soviet passports and the census did record natsionalnost. That is not the empirical problem with nationalism-as-primary-cause accounts.

choices.text: No Soviet republic ever held a referendum on independence, so there is no popular-opinion evidence either way
choices.note: Several referenda were held — the March 1991 all-Union referendum, the December 1991 Ukrainian independence referendum, Baltic and Georgian independence votes, and others. The empirical record on popular support is actually rich; the problem is that it doesn't fit a slow-nationalist-buildup story.

choices.text: Nationalism as an explanation must be rejected because all Soviet republics became single-party Communist states after 1991, showing no nationalist dimension to post-Soviet politics
choices.note: Post-Soviet republics did not uniformly become single-party Communist states. The empirical problem is about the unlikely career histories of 1991's 'nationalist' leaders and the sudden flip in public opinion, not about a nonexistent uniform communist outcome.

questions.explanation: A careful look at 1991 pushes back against any simple 'long-repressed nationalism burst through' story. Twelve of the fifteen republic leaders at collapse had been longtime Party members, and ten were career apparatchiks; leaders like Uzbekistan's Karimov, Turkmenistan's Niyazov, and Ukraine's Kravchuk had until the very eve of independence crushed or sidelined local nationalist movements. In some places (Latvia, Estonia, parts of Ukraine) ethnic Russians actually backed local sovereignty. And the March 1991 all-Union referendum showed 70-plus percent of voters still wanted to preserve the union — yet within months Ukraine's December 1991 referendum produced roughly 90 percent in favor of independence. That speed of reversal makes nationalism convincing as a mobilizational vehicle once the system started slipping, but not as the deep cause of the collapse itself.

questions.prompt: The December 1995 Russian State Duma elections produced which headline result?

choices.text: The KPRF under Zyuganov topped the party-list vote at about 22 percent; only four of 43 parties cleared the 5 percent threshold (KPRF, LDPR, Our Home Is Russia, Yabloko)
Yegor Gaidar's Russia's Democratic Choice won a commanding plurality, entrenching the 'shock therapy' reform camp as Russia's dominant political force
choices.note: Russia's Democratic Choice failed to clear the 5 percent threshold in 1995. The reform camp was fragmented and the Communist Party won the headline result.

choices.text: Zhirinovsky's LDPR repeated its 1993 first-place finish with about 23 percent, becoming the Duma's single largest faction
choices.note: LDPR came second, with about 11 percent, less than half its 1993 share. The 1995 surprise was the Communist recovery, not another LDPR surge.

choices.text: No party cleared the 5 percent threshold and the Duma's party-list seats went unfilled, producing an all-single-mandate parliament
choices.note: Four parties cleared the 5 percent threshold in 1995. A total-failure scenario did not occur.

questions.explanation: The 1995 Duma election was the KPRF's post-Soviet high-water mark. With Yeltsin's popularity at single-digit approval by September 1995, Zyuganov's revived Communist Party took about 22 percent of the party-list vote; Zhirinovsky's LDPR came second at about 11 percent; Prime Minister Viktor Chernomyrdin's Kremlin-built 'Our Home Is Russia' was third at about 10 percent; and Yavlinsky's Yabloko edged above 5 percent. Forty-three parties competed, and the 5 percent threshold knocked all but four out of list-seat allocation — nearly half of party-list votes went to parties that won no list seats, a pathology that drove later debates about recalibrating the threshold. The result set up the widespread fear heading into the 1996 presidential election that Zyuganov would beat Yeltsin and reverse the market transition.

questions.prompt: What was the headline political outcome of Ukraine's March-April 1994 Verkhovna Rada elections?

choices.text: A fragmented 450-seat Rada: KPU topped the party-aligned vote at ~13 percent, Rukh was second at ~5.5 percent of seats, and a majority of deputies sat as independents
An outright majority for Rukh and the national-democratic bloc, which immediately set the Rada on a pro-Western reform course
choices.note: Rukh came second and never approached a Rada majority; it won roughly 5.5 percent of seats. The left bloc (KPU plus allies), not the national-democrats, dominated the 1994 Rada.

choices.text: A sweeping plurality for a centrist Kuchma-aligned party of power, which gave Kuchma a working parliamentary majority for his July 1994 presidential run
choices.note: No Kuchma-aligned party-of-power existed in 1994; Kuchma campaigned for the presidency against Kravchuk without a parliamentary vehicle. The Rada itself was dominated by the KPU and by independents.

choices.text: A boycott by all opposition parties, leaving only a single-candidate Communist slate, so the new Rada consisted entirely of KPU deputies
choices.note: The 1994 Rada vote was a genuinely contested multi-party election. The KPU topped the party-aligned vote but took only about 13 percent; 15 parties won seats and many deputies were independents.

questions.explanation: The party-aligned portion of the 1994 Rada was led by the freshly re-legalized Communist Party of Ukraine at about 13 percent, followed by Rukh at about 5.5 percent of seats. Fifteen parties won seats in total, and a majority of the 450 deputies identified with no party at all. The allied left bloc (KPU, Socialists, Peasants) was the dominant coordinated force. That fragmentation constrained both Kravchuk and, after July 1994, Kuchma, and contributed to the political pressure that produced a new mixed electoral system for 1998.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What was the headline political outcome of Ukraine's March 1998 Verkhovna Rada elections?

choices.text: The KPU came first at ~25 percent of the list vote (~27 percent of seats), Rukh second at ~10 percent, and the left bloc remained dominant in a fragmented Rada
A sweeping victory for Leonid Kuchma's party 'For United Ukraine,' which won an absolute majority and gave Kuchma a reliable pro-presidential parliamentary bloc
choices.note: 'For United Ukraine' is a 2002 pro-Kuchma bloc, not a 1998 formation. In 1998 the KPU and the broader left bloc topped the results; Kuchma's authority rested on the presidency, not a party majority.

choices.text: A breakthrough for Yulia Tymoshenko's bloc, which came first on the list vote and placed Tymoshenko in the chair of the Rada
choices.note: Tymoshenko's eponymous bloc (BYuT) is a later formation and did not top the 1998 results. The KPU was the list-vote winner in 1998.

choices.text: A left-wing-parties boycott that left Rukh with an outright majority and triggered a constitutional amendment banning communism
choices.note: No left-wing boycott occurred, Rukh did not win a majority, and no ban on communism passed at the time. Ukraine's formal decommunization laws came only in 2015, after the Maidan.

questions.explanation: The 1998 Rada elections confirmed the KPU as the dominant 1990s Ukrainian party: it took about 25 percent of the party-list vote and about 27 percent of seats, with Rukh second at about 10 percent on the list. Centrist, oligarch-linked, and regional 'party-of-power' formations captured many of the single-mandate seats, alongside large numbers of independents. The result was a parliament where the left could often block reform but could not govern, while Kuchma navigated through shifting single-mandate-district coalitions — a pattern that shaped the run-up to the 1999 presidential election and the later Kuchmagate crisis.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: The Russian Federation Treaty of 31 March 1992 is best described as:

choices.text: Three parallel agreements between the federal government and each category of federal subject, setting somewhat different terms; Tatarstan and Chechnya refused to sign, and Tatarstan joined separately in 1994
A single document granting every federal subject an identical constitutional right to secede from the Russian Federation
choices.note: The Federation Treaty did not grant a secession right — that was a Soviet-level feature of union republics that the Russian Federation explicitly did not replicate. Differences across categories of subject were about competences and revenue, not exit rights.

choices.text: A Yeltsin decree abolishing all ethnic republics and replacing them with uniform 'federal provinces' modeled on the United States
choices.note: The Federation Treaty preserved, not abolished, the distinction between ethnic republics and other federal subjects. Uniform 'federal provinces' on an American model were never adopted.

choices.text: A 1992 UN-sponsored accord in which foreign observers arbitrated disputes between Moscow and rebellious regions; it was rejected by both Russia and the UN General Assembly
choices.note: The Federation Treaty was a purely Russian internal arrangement. No UN role, no arbitration mechanism, no rejection at the General Assembly.

questions.explanation: Signed on 31 March 1992, the Federation Treaty was actually a bundle of three agreements between the federal center and each category of subject. It formalized the post-Soviet federal structure and was incorporated (in modified form) into the 1993 Constitution. Two republics refused to sign in 1992: Chechnya (which had declared independence under Dudayev in November 1991) and Tatarstan (which under Shaimiev sought better terms than the republics' template offered). Chechnya continued to pursue secession; Tatarstan ultimately entered the federation through a separate, more generous bilateral power-sharing treaty signed in February 1994 — the template for the roughly forty additional bilateral treaties Yeltsin signed with individual subjects through 1998, which together defined 1990s 'asymmetric federalism.'
sources: General post-Soviet politics literature (institutional histories of 1990s Russian federalism)

questions.prompt: How were the heads of Russia's oblasts and krays (governors) selected across the 1990s?

choices.text: Initially appointed by Yeltsin under 1991 decree powers, then popularly elected after Yeltsin formalized nationwide gubernatorial elections for December 1996
Popularly elected from the start of the Russian Federation in 1991-92, under a direct-election rule written into the 1993 Constitution
choices.note: The 1993 Constitution did not entrench direct gubernatorial elections. Yeltsin appointed governors under decree powers in the early 1990s; direct elections were formalized only from 1996 onward.

choices.text: Elected indirectly by regional legislatures throughout the 1990s; direct popular election of governors only began under Putin in 2000
choices.note: The opposite of the actual trajectory. Indirect legislative selection was not the 1990s norm, and direct elections of governors existed before Putin — his 2004 reform abolished them rather than creating them.

choices.text: Elected in ethnic republics from 1991 but never elected in oblasts and krays during the 1990s; oblast governors remained Yeltsin appointees until Putin introduced elections in 2001
choices.note: The first part is close to right — republic presidents were elected locally in the early 1990s — but the rest is backwards: most oblast and kray governors had been elected by the late 1990s, and Putin's 2004 reform ended direct elections rather than introducing them.

questions.explanation: In the ethnic republics, presidents were elected locally from the early 1990s, giving their incumbents legitimacy independent of Moscow. In oblasts and krays, by contrast, Yeltsin initially appointed governors under decree powers the Congress of People's Deputies granted him in November 1991. A few oblast-level gubernatorial elections started in 1995 (Sverdlovsk's Eduard Rossel, fired by Yeltsin and then voted back in, was an emblematic case), and Yeltsin formalized nationwide direct gubernatorial elections for December 1996. By the late 1990s Russia had an unusual hybrid: directly elected governors across both ethnic and non-ethnic subjects, bargaining bilaterally with a weakened Moscow. Putin's post-Beslan 2004 reform abolished direct gubernatorial elections — the main lever behind his 'power vertical' centralization — before a limited, filtered version was reintroduced in 2012.

questions.prompt: How did the electoral system used for Ukraine's 1994 Verkhovna Rada elections differ from the one used in 1998?

choices.text: 1994 used single-mandate majority-runoff; 1998 used a mixed system — half by PR with a 4 percent threshold, half by single-mandate district
1994 used a mixed PR+SMD system; 1998 moved to a fully single-mandate system with no party-list tier
choices.note: The direction of change is reversed. 1994 was all single-mandate (majority-runoff); 1998 was the first election to introduce the PR tier.

choices.text: Both 1994 and 1998 used pure proportional representation with a 5 percent threshold and closed national lists
choices.note: Pure PR was not adopted until 2006. In 1994 the system was all single-mandate; in 1998 it was mixed PR (with a 4 percent threshold) plus SMD.

choices.text: 1994 used a multi-member plurality system; 1998 used a majority-runoff system — both without any party-list component
choices.note: The 1994 system was single-mandate majority-runoff (not multi-member). 1998 introduced a nationwide PR tier; it was not a pure runoff system.

questions.explanation: Ukraine's 1994 Rada elections ran under the Soviet-era single-mandate majority-runoff rules, with a 50-percent turnout threshold that left many districts requiring repeat rounds. After the 1996 Constitution, Ukraine adopted a mixed formula for 1998: half of the 450 seats elected by closed-list proportional representation with a 4 percent threshold, half by single-mandate district. The shift opened a clearer channel for national parties (notably the KPU and Rukh, which were organized enough to take advantage of the list tier) while preserving a large block of SMD seats where oligarch-linked incumbents and independents continued to dominate.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States
